title: The While Loop 🎡
description: Keep spinning as long as a condition is true.

slides.title: The Merry-Go-Round 🎡
slides.content:
A **while** loop is the simplest loop.

It is exactly like an `if` statement, except instead of running the code block just once, it runs it **over and over** as long as the condition remains true.

slides.title: While Syntax 🔄
slides.content:
Look at this structure. 

1. It checks if `count < 3`.
2. If true, prints the phrase, then adds 1 to `count`.
3. Then it jumps back to the TOP and asks the question again.

quizData.question: How many times does the word "Looping..." print in the code above?
quizData.explanation: It prints when count is 0, 1, and 2. When count becomes 3, the check (3 < 3) is false, so it stops. Total: 3 times.

slides.title: The Vital Piece: The Update 🔧
slides.content:
Did you notice the line `count = count + 1;`?

If we didn't update the `count` variable, `count < 3` would NEVER be false. It would be `0 < 3` forever.

Every time you write a while loop, you MUST include a way for the condition to eventually become false.

quizData.question: What happens if a while loop's condition never becomes false?

options.text: It runs exactly 10 times and stops.
It runs forever, crashing or freezing the program.

quizData.explanation: This is called an Infinite Loop. The computer will just keep executing the block until it runs out of memory or you forcefully kill it!

slides.title: The ++ Shortcut 🏎️
slides.content:
Because adding 1 to a variable is so common in loops, Java gives us a shortcut. 

Instead of `count = count + 1;`, you can simply write `count++;`.

quizData.question: If `x = 5`, what will `x` be after calling `x++;` twice?
quizData.explanation: The ++ operator adds exactly 1. Calling it twice adds 2. 5 + 2 = 7.
